points: Multiples are products of a number (e.g., 15 is a common multiple of 3 and 5), while factors are numbers that divide it exactly.
Common factors of two numbers are the divisors shared by both, used to simplify fractions and find jump sizes in the treasure game.
A perfect number is one where the sum of its divisors equals twice the number itself (e.g., 6 and 28).
Prime numbers have exactly two factors (1 and themselves), whereas composite numbers have more than two factors.
The number 1 is unique as it is neither prime nor composite. 2 is the only even prime number in existence.
Twin primes are pairs of prime numbers with a difference of exactly 2, such as (3, 5) or (17, 19).
Two numbers are co-prime if their only common factor is 1, regardless of whether the numbers themselves are prime or composite.
The first common multiple (LCM) of two co-prime numbers is always equal to their product ($l \times b$).
Co-prime relationships are vital for security (safekeeping treasures) and artistic patterns like thread art.
Prime factorisation is the unique way of writing any number greater than 1 as a product of prime numbers.
The order of factors doesn't change the result, but listing them in increasing order is a standard convention.
It is used to verify co-primality (no shared prime factors) and divisibility (one factorisation contained in another).
Divisibility by 10, 5, and 2 can be checked by looking at the last digit (0, 0/5, or even digits respectively).
For 4 and 8, one must check the number formed by the last two or last three digits of the original number.
These rules allow for checking factors of large numbers without performing tedious long division.
